args.prompt:
Ingest the following foundational overview of biosemiotics and build a concept graph capturing its main claims, distinctions, and the relations between them.

CORPUS: "The Living Sign: A Foundational Overview of Biosemiotics"

1. THE BIOLOGICAL PARADIGM SHIFT: FROM MACHINES TO INTERPRETERS
Biosemiotics is an ontological departure from the Newtonian/mechanical view of biology. Under the old paradigm, the organism is a sophisticated automaton — a collection of chemical "cogs" and "gears" driven by thermodynamic necessity. Biosemiotics (drawing on Peirce, in the Kuhnian sense) proposes a "new paradigm" identifying the production and interpretation of signs as the fundamental characteristic of life. Life is not merely a set of reactions; it is a relational system of meaning. Survival is not just the avoidance of entropy but the continuous act of interpretation — navigating a world of meaning, not just matter. This necessitates investigating the fundamental "building block" of life's relational system: the Sign.

2. ANATOMY OF MEANING: SAUSSURE VS. PEIRCE
Two models of the sign. Saussurean Dyad: two-part relationship between Signifier (sound/image) and Signified (concept); primarily arbitrary and linguistic; meaning through difference. Peircean Triad: triadic process of Representamen (form), Object (referent), and Interpretant (effect); rooted in logic and semiosis; includes iconic, indexical, and symbolic modes. The Interpretant is the "proper significate effect" — in biology, NOT a mental concept but a physiological one (protein folding, metabolic shift, behavioral flight). Peirce's three modes: Iconic (meaning through similarity, e.g., butterfly wing mimicking predator's eye); Indexical (meaning through real connection / cause-effect, e.g., pheromone trail indexing food, smoke indexing fire); Symbolic (meaning through established interpretive habit or code, e.g., the genetic code mapping codons to amino acids). The triadic sign requires an agent to enact interpretation.

3. THE ORGANISM AS AUTONOMOUS AGENT
Stuart Kauffman: organisms as "autonomous agents" acting on their own behalf. From a semiotic perspective this agency manifests as: (a) Self-Interpretation: the life cycle is a temporal unfolding of semiosis; the system reads its own state to maintain its "self"; (b) Organisms as Texts: drawing on Derrida's "écriture"/writing, the organism is a "self-reading text" whose survival depends on organic codes translating internal genetic instructions into external physiological reality; (c) Pragmatism: meaning is defined by utility; a sign is a biological adaptation with useful function; if a signal leads to a survival-enhancing action (successful interpretant), it has biological meaning.

4. MAPPING THE UMWELT
Jakob von Uexküll: Umwelt is the subjective semiotic world of an organism — not the "objective" environment (Umgebung) but a filtered reality constructed through the organism's specific sensors and needs. (a) Subjective Interpretation: animals are active interpreters, not passive recipients (tick: world of light and heat; human: vastly different). (b) Semiotic Wholeness: Umwelt unites sensory, metabolic, and behavioral semiotic processes into a functional whole. (c) Agency as Filter: the organism's biological needs define what becomes a "sign" in its world; the environment is "diacritic" — a system of differences where only what is relevant to the agent is visible.

5. THE SEMIOSPHERE
Juri Lotman: the Semiosphere is the semiotic equivalent of the biosphere — the space where all signs are created, shared, and embedded. Definition: the set of all interconnected Umwelts; the total "semiotic space" of nature. Function: the vital space that makes semiosis possible; the site where information is translated across species boundaries. So-what: nature is a site of knowledge; meaning and interpretation are not exclusive to humans; the earth is a global network of non-human "knowers" exchanging information.

6. THE CODE OF LIFE: INFORMATION VS MATTER
A distinction between the material carrier of life and the information it encodes. Through post-structuralism and Derrida's "writing": Genotype = the Sign (system of information or "writing," a distribution of signifying forms whose meaning is contingent on cellular context); DNA = the Medium (material carrier — the chemical "ink" and "paper"). Protein synthesis = the biological act of reading: cellular machinery interprets genetic "writing" to produce physiological effect, paralleling a reader interpreting a text to produce a mental effect. Symbiotic evolution as semiotic process: different species "read" and co-author each other's genetic codes over eons.

7. CONCLUSION
Biosemiotics: biological adaptation is more than physical struggle for survival — it is an ongoing semiotic process of self-interpretation. From the "diacritic writing" of the genetic code to the vast interconnected semiosphere, life is defined not by its chemical constituents but by its capacity to create, interpret, and persist through signs. Life is the temporal unfolding of meaning — a symphony where every living thing is simultaneously the message, the messenger, and the interpreter.

Treat this as the working corpus. Build concept communities around: (i) the mechanical-paradigm baseline, (ii) Peircean triadic sign and its three modes, (iii) organism-as-autonomous-agent / self-reading text, (iv) Umwelt as subjective filter, (v) Semiosphere as global sign-space, (vi) the genetic code as écriture / genotype-vs-DNA distinction, and (vii) the broader paradigm-shift claim (mechanism vs semiosis). Track inter-community bridges (e.g., between Peircean interpretant and Kauffmanian agency; between Umwelt and Semiosphere; between Derrida's writing and the genetic code; between the three Peircean modes and the three layers: cell / organism / ecosystem).
